content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 19, 1991


Ms. Teresa Beck
Counsel, Judiciary Committee
Mississippi House of Representatives
P.O. Box 1018
Jackson, Mississippi  39215-1018

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. G-91-520

Dear Ms. Beck:

Your letter of November 1, 1991 requests information as to
California law regarding the employment by governmental entities
of legislators who are attorneys.

The Political Reform Act, the body of law administered by the
Fair Political Practices Commission, does not address the issue of
the permissibility of such employment.  The question would be
considered a matter covered by the law pertaining to "incompatible
activities," and would be under the jurisdiction of the Office of
the Attorney General.

For your convenience and information, we are enclosing a copy
of a publication entitled "Conflicts of Interests," which is
produced and distributed by the Attorney General's Office.
Chapter VI deals with the "Legislative Code of Ethics" found in
the California Government Code, Section 8920 et seq.  We refer you
specifically to Government Code Section 8920(b), discussed on
pages 39 and 40 of the enclosed booklet for the information you
are seeking.

With regard to the Political Reform Act, legislators are
subject to conflicts of interest as delineated in Government Code
Sections 87102.5-87102.6, but the thrust of those statutes is to
prohibit participation by a legislator in particular kinds of
governmental decisions in which the legislator knows or has reason
to know that he or she has a financial interest.  Subsequent to
leaving office, a legislator would be subject to the "revolving


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. G-91-520
Page 2


door" provision, found in Government Code Section 87406(b), which
would restrict the former legislator's employment. In the event
that the Political Reform Act provisions could be useful to you,
we are also enclosing a copy of the current Act.

I hope that the enclosed publications will assist you in your
research. If you have further questions regarding incompatible
activities, please contact the Office of the California Attorney
General, 1515 K Street, Suite 600, P.O. Box 944255, Sacramento,
California 94244-2550. If you have questions regarding the
Political Reform Act, please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin /
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Susan L. Morrow
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:ken

sections.question_synthetic: May governmental entities employ legislators who are attorneys, and what California law governs such employment?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not address this issue; it falls under 'incompatible activities' jurisdiction of the California Attorney General's office. Legislators are subject to conflicts of interest provisions under Government Code Sections 87102.5-87102.6 and the 'revolving door' provision under Section 87406(b) after leaving office.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a correspondence/advice letter from FPPC to an out-of-state requester. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections with headers are present in the document. The letter provides legal guidance and referral rather than a formal opinion. File number G-91-520 is referenced. Minor OCR issues noted in source document (e.g., 'P01itical' for 'Political', 'Decenber' for 'December').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May governmental entities employ legislators who are attorneys, and what California law governs such employment?

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not address this issue; it falls under 'incompatible activities' jurisdiction of the California Attorney General's office. Legislators are subject to conflicts of interest provisions under Government Code Sections 87102.5-87102.6 and the 'revolving door' provision under Section 87406(b) after leaving office.

In the event that the Political Reform Act provisions could be useful to you, we are also enclosing a copy of the current Act. I hope that the enclosed publications will assist you in your research. embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Mississippi House Judiciary Committee counsel requesting information about California law regarding employment of attorney-legislators by governmental entities, declining jurisdiction and referring the matter to the California Attorney General's office as an incompatible activities issue.
